headline: WATTAL2: The Sequel Nobody Asked For That Everybody Needs
subhead: Drake and Future's studio session photo is doing more cultural work than most albums dropped this year.

body.text: One photo. No tracklist. No release date. And the internet fractured anyway. That's the specific gravity Drake and Future carry when they move together — even a hint of a sequel to What A Time To Be Alive sends the culture into a controlled delirium that no marketing budget can manufacture.

According to a Complex report citing an Instagram studio session photo, Drake and Future are hinting at a follow-up to their 2015 collaborative mixtape, with a Metro Boomin reference threaded into the image's orbit. That's the full inventory of confirmed facts. And yet here we are — because that's how legacy works. When two artists have already built a mythology together, the rumor of a reunion isn't just news, it's an event. The original WATTAL dropped in 72 hours, debuted at number one, and essentially codified a whole aesthetic of trap-infused melodic rap that half the genre still hasn't climbed out from under.

You have to understand what What A Time To Be Alive actually was before you can understand why its sequel is culturally loaded. It wasn't supposed to be a classic. It was a weekend project that dropped like a surprise sneaker release — no runway, no press cycle, just a link in the bio and a timestamp. And because it moved that way, it hit like something rare. The lo-fi urgency of it, the shared aesthetic laziness-as-flex, the Metro Boomin production holding the whole thing together like a spine — all of it read as something authentic precisely because it wasn't engineered to perform.

That's the paradox every sequel faces. The original's power came from its casualness. The moment you announce Part Two, you've already made it formal. You've told the audience to prepare. And prepared audiences are different animals than audiences who get hit cold. So before a single bar is written — or at least before any bar is confirmed — WATTAL2 is already fighting against its own mythology. It has to be good enough to justify its existence and casual enough to recapture the spirit that made the first one land. That's a needle-thread few artists ever manage.

And then there's the Metro Boomin variable. The original was a Metro joint in DNA if not exclusively in execution — his production fingerprint was all over that project's tone. The fact that his name surfaced in connection with this studio session photo isn't a footnote. It's the structural argument for why this sequel has a real shot at landing. Metro isn't just a producer at this point; he's a genre-defining architect who has demonstrated, repeatedly, that he understands how to make rappers of different frequencies coexist on the same sonic landscape without either one losing altitude.
The original's power came from its casualness. The moment you announce Part Two, you've already made it formal.

Let's talk producer economics for a second, because this is where casual listeners and actual students of the craft diverge. Metro Boomin being in the conversation around this project isn't just a vibe confirmation — it's a structural decision. Producers on collaborative albums between two high-profile MCs function as referees as much as architects. They establish the sonic neutral ground where neither artist's ego dominates the track. They create the container. When that container is built by someone both artists trust and both artists have individually excelled over, you reduce the friction that usually makes collab albums feel like two separate solo records stitched together at the seams.

Think about what Metro has done in rooms with rappers who have massive individual gravity. He doesn't flatten anyone's style — he finds the register where their strengths become complementary rather than competitive. Future's cadence, that underwater drawl layered over hard 808s, and Drake's melodic precision and lyrical self-consciousness are genuinely different instruments. On paper they shouldn't sit in the same frequency. In practice, when the production is right, they create a tension that feels cinematic — like two characters in the same film who operate by different rules but share the same world. Metro Boomin understands that world.

Here's the angle most coverage is missing: this isn't just about whether the project is good. It's about what it signals for how legacy artists are choosing to deploy their cultural capital in a moment when the album format itself is under structural pressure. Streaming economics have made the album less financially critical than it was in 2015. Playlist placement, individual single performance, sync deals, touring — the P&L of a major artist in 2025 doesn't rise and fall on album sales the way it once did. So when artists of this caliber choose to make albums — especially collaborative albums — it's a statement about craft and legacy, not commerce.
Legacy Moves in the Streaming Era

That's actually what makes a potential WATTAL sequel more interesting than anything surface-level about the Drake-Future dynamic. Both artists have reached a phase in their careers where catalog matters as much as current output. Future's body of work — the Pluto era, the HNDRXX run, the self-titled record, the DS2 legacy — already constitutes a canonical argument. Drake's catalog conversation is louder, messier, more contested, and more scrutinized than virtually any artist alive. A collaborative project between them at this stage isn't just music — it's archival work. It's both of them deciding how they want to be remembered in the same breath.

The original WATTAL gets cited more and more as a document of a specific cultural moment — late Obama era, trap at the peak of its mainstream penetration, Drake at maximum radius, Future coming off the emotional and commercial high of DS2. Whatever a sequel captures, it will inevitably be read as a document of its own moment too. Which raises the question nobody is asking right now but will be asking the day it drops: what does 2025 or 2026 actually sound like? What are these artists saying to each other and to the culture when the cultural context has shifted so dramatically — when trap has been digested and exported globally, when both artists have navigated public battles of various kinds, when the streaming landscape has atomized attention in ways that didn't exist when the first one landed?

A studio photo and a Metro Boomin reference don't answer any of that. They just open the door. The real conversation — about what this project might be structurally, where it fits in both artists' arc, what producer involvement signals about sonic direction, and what the indie and major label economics of a project like this look like in the current landscape — that conversation is longer than any Instagram caption will ever be. And it goes deeper than what most outlets are set up to hold.

body.headline: Want the deeper read?
body.body: We publish the full breakdown — the names, the receipts, the predictions — on the Sneakz & Beatz Substack. Free to subscribe. New essays land Tuesdays and Fridays.
body.cta_label: Read the full version on Substack

tldr: Drake and Future are hinting at a sequel to What A Time To Be Alive, with a Metro Boomin reference circling the studio session photo. PHRHX breaks down why this is bigger than hype — and what it means for legacy, craft, and the economics of collaborative rap in 2025.

source.title: Drake and Future Tease Joint Album Number Two
